---
title: RIP pandas, welcome Polars
frontpic: img/polars_vs_pandas.png
frontpicwidth: 40%
frontpicmargintop: 30px
frontpicmarginbottom: 20px
author: Marie-Hélène Burle
date: 2026-04-7
date-format: long
execute:
  freeze: auto
  cache: true
  error: true
  echo: true
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: [default, ../revealjspdpl.scss]
    logo: /img/favicon_sfudrac.png
    highlight-style: vim-dark
    code-line-numbers: false
    template-partials:
      - ../title-slide.html
    pointer:
      color: "#b5111b"
      pointerSize: 32
    link-external-newwindow: true
    footer: <a href="wb_polars2.qmd"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="16" height="16" fill="rgb(32,118,193)" class="bi bi-arrow-90deg-up" viewBox="0 0 16 16"><path fill-rule="evenodd" d="M4.854 1.146a.5.5 0 0 0-.708 0l-4 4a.5.5 0 1 0 .708.708L4 2.707V12.5A2.5 2.5 0 0 0 6.5 15h8a.5.5 0 0 0 0-1h-8A1.5 1.5 0 0 1 5 12.5V2.707l3.146 3.147a.5.5 0 1 0 .708-.708l-4-4z"/></svg>&nbsp;Back to webinar page</a>
    auto-stretch: false
revealjs-plugins:
  - pointer
---

## Goal of this webinar {.center}

There is a new and much better library for Python DataFrames

There are no downsides to using it, beside the effort of changing habits. For new users who don't have habits yet, there are no downsides

Yet, all Python intro courses still teach the old tool

In this webinar, I will not teach Polars and its syntax. Instead I will demo why it is better

[My goal is to help shift the culture towards a wider adoption of Polars instead of pandas]{.emph}

## A brief history of DataFrames {.center}

```{dot}
//| echo: false
//| fig-height: 450px

strict graph {

bgcolor="transparent"
graph [fontname="Inconsolata, sans-serif"]
node [fontname="Inconsolata, sans-serif", fontsize=15]

y1 [label=1990, shape=plaintext, group=g1, group=g1]
y2 [label=2000, shape=plaintext, group=g1, group=g1]
y3 [label=2008, shape=plaintext, group=g1]
y4 [label=2020, shape=plaintext, group=g1]

l1 [label="S language introduces concept of DataFrames", href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S_(programming_language)", shape=plaintext, group=g2]
l2 [label="R supercedes S and inherits DataFrames", href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/R_(programming_language)", shape=plaintext, group=g2]
l3 [label="DataFrames ported to Python with pandas", href="https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas", shape=plaintext, group=g2, fontcolor="#e84c44"]
l4 [label="Better DataFrames library for Python: Polars", href="https://github.com/pola-rs/polars", shape=plaintext, group=g2, fontcolor="#69c9e6"]

{rank=same; y1 l1}

y1 -- y2 -- y3 -- y4
l1 -- l2 -- l3 -- l4 [style=invis]

}
```

## Overview {.center}

| | ![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="55%"} || ![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="50%"} |
|--|--|--|--|
| Available for | Python || Rust, Python, R, NodeJS |
| Written in | Cython || Rust |
| Multithreading | Some operations || Yes (GIL released) |
| Index | Rows are indexed || Integer positions are used |
| Evaluation | Eager || Eager and lazy |
| Query optimizer | No || Yes |
| Out-of-core | No || Yes |
| [SIMD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_instruction,_multiple_data) vectorization | Yes || Yes |
| Data in memory | [NumPy](https://github.com/numpy/numpy) arrays || [Apache Arrow](https://github.com/apache/arrow) arrays |
| Memory efficiency | Poor || Excellent |
| Missing data handling | Inconsistent || Consistent; type stability |

## Missing data {.center}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="52%"}

**pandas**

For historical reasons, depending on dtype, missing values are one of:

- `NaN` (`numpy.nan`, dtype float)
- `None`
- `NaT` (Not-a-Time, dtype datetime)
- `pandas.NA` (nullable scalar)

This leads to inconsistent behaviour and unexpected dtype changes

[Ongoing open issue since 2020](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/32265)

:::

:::{.column width="2%"}
:::

:::{.column width="46%"}

**Polars**

One single missing value: `null`

Simple and consistent behaviour across all data types and better performance

`NaN` exists and is a float. It is **not** missing data, but the result of mathematical operations that don't return numbers

[Here](https://www.dontusethiscode.com/blog/2025-01-22_null_vs_nan_applied.html) is a blog that goes into this in details

:::

::::

## Group by {.center}

[Here](https://labs.quansight.org/blog/dataframe-group-by) is a great blog comparing the ease of use of passing expressions to groups in Polars compared to pandas

*TL;DR Polars has a much more straightforward and efficient syntax*

## A note on my comparisons {.center}

The performance of tools can only be evaluated on optimized code: poorly written pandas code is a lot slower than efficient pandas code

To ensure fairness towards pandas, I use the best pandas code from [Alex Razoumov pandas course](https://wgpages.netlify.app/python2/python-13-pandas/) in which he benchmarks various syntaxes

# Example 1: a classic

## FizzBuzz {.center}

[FizzBuzz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fizz_buzz#Programming) is a programming exercise based on a children game which consists of counting to `n`, replacing:

- any number divisible by `3` with `Fizz`
- any number divisible by `5` with `Buzz`
- any number divisible by both `3` and `5` with `FizzBuzz`

Let's do it:

```{python}
# Load libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import polars as pl

# Set series length
n = 10_000
```

## Code {.center}

![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
df_pd = pd.DataFrame()

df_pd["Count"] = np.arange(1, n+1)

df_pd["FizzBuzz"] = df_pd["Count"].astype(str)

df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 3 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Fizz"
df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 5 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Buzz"
df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 15 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "FizzBuzz"
```
## {.center}

```{python}
df_pd
```

## Code {.center}

![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)})

df_pl.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz"))

df_pl = df_pl.with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz"))
    .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz"))
    .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz"))
    .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz")
)
```

## {.center}

```{python}
df_pl
```

## Code {.center}

![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
#| code-line-numbers: "1-10|12"

df_pl_lazy = pl.LazyFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)})

df_pl_lazy.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz"))

df_pl_lazy = df_pl_lazy.with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz"))
    .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz"))
    .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz"))
    .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz")
)

df_pl_lazy_collected = df_pl_lazy.collect()
```

## {.center}

```{python}
df_pl_lazy_collected
```

## Timing {.center}

![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
%%timeit

df_pd = pd.DataFrame()

df_pd["Count"] = np.arange(1, n+1)

df_pd["FizzBuzz"] = df_pd["Count"].astype(str)

df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 3 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Fizz"
df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 5 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "Buzz"
df_pd.loc[df_pd["Count"] % 15 == 0, "FizzBuzz"] = "FizzBuzz"
```

## Timing {.center}

![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
%%timeit

df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)})

df_pl.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz"))

df_pl.with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz"))
    .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz"))
    .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz"))
    .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz")
)
```

## Timing {.center}

![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
%%timeit

df_pl_lazy = pl.LazyFrame({"Count": np.arange(1, n+1)})

df_pl_lazy.with_columns(pl.col("Count").cast(pl.String).alias("FizzBuzz"))

df_pl_lazy = df_pl_lazy.with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("Count") % 3 == 0).then(pl.lit("Fizz"))
    .when(pl.col("Count") % 5 == 0).then(pl.lit("Buzz"))
    .when(pl.col("Count") % 15 == 0).then(pl.lit("FizzBuzz"))
    .otherwise(pl.col("Count")).alias("FizzBuzz")
)

df_pl = df_pl_lazy.collect()
```

## Speedup {.center}

Speedup with Polars DataFrame around **6** \
With Polars LazyFrame around **8**

These speedups highly depend on the types and sizes of the problems

How much faster Polars is does vary, what doesn't vary is that it is always much faster

## Memory usage {.center}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="32%"}

**pandas**

```{python}
df_pd.memory_usage()
```

132 + 80,000 * 2 = \
160,132 bytes \
**≈ 160 KB**

:::

:::{.column width="2%"}
:::

:::{.column width="32%"}

**Polars**

```{python}
df_pl.estimated_size()
```

119,409 bytes \
**≈ 119 KB**

160,132 / 119,409 ≈ 1.3 \
Footprint 1.3 times smaller

:::

:::{.column width="2%"}
:::

:::{.column width="32%"}

**Polars Lazy API**

Same as Polars eager: \
if you collect the entire DataFrame as we did here, the lazy API does not reduce the memory footprint

:::

::::

# Example 2: medium dataset

## Data characteristics {.center}

Shape: 216,930 rows, 7 columns

## Code {.center}

![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
df_pd = pd.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv"
)

df_pd_subset = df_pd.loc[df_pd["Category"] == "HISTORY"]

df_pd_subset.shape
```

## Code {.center}

![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
df_pl = pl.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv"
)

df_pl_subset = df_pl.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY")

df_pl_subset.shape
```

## Code {.center}

![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv"
)

df_pl_lazy_subset_collected = df_pl_lazy.filter(
    pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY"
).collect()

df_pl_lazy_subset_collected.shape
```

## Timing {.center}

![](img/logo_pandas_full.svg){width="10%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
%%timeit

df_pd = pd.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv"
)

df_pd.loc[df_pd["Category"] == "HISTORY"].shape
```

## Timing {.center}

![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
%%timeit

df_pl = pl.read_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv"
)

df_pl.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").shape
```

## Timing {.center}

![](img/logo_polars_full.svg){width="12%" fig-align="right"}

```{python}
%%timeit

df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv(
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/razoumov/publish/master/jeopardy.csv"
)

df_pl_lazy.filter(pl.col("Category") == "HISTORY").collect().shape
```

## Speedup {.center}

Polars here brings a speedup around **1.5**

The Lazy API ramps that up to a speedup of **17**

## Memory usage {.center}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="30%"}

```{python}
df_pd.memory_usage()
```

132 + 1,735,440 * 7 = \
12,148,212 bytes \
**≈ 12 MB**

:::

:::{.column width="2%"}
:::

:::{.column width="33%"}

```{python}
df_pl.estimated_size()
```

31,606,250 bytes \
**≈ 31 MB**

31,606,250 / 12,148,212 ≈ 2.6 \
Footprint 2.6 times *bigger*

:::

:::{.column width="2%"}
:::

:::{.column width="33%"}

```{python}
df_pl_lazy_subset_collected.estimated_size()
```

48,774 bytes \
**≈ 49 KB**

12,148,212 / 48,774 ≈ 249 \
Footprint **249 times smaller**

If you only collect a subset of the data, the memory footprint drops dramatically

:::

::::

# Example 3:<br>data too big to fit in memory

## Example dataset {.center}

Let's play with data from the [GBIF](https://www.gbif.org/) website for free and open access to biodiversity data

The [Southern African Bird Atlas Project 2](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/906e6978-e292-4a8b-9c39-adf6bb0f3323) contains a raw CSV file of 42 GB and an interpreted CSV file of 12.3 GB. Let's look at the latter:

Shape: 25,687,526 rows, 50 columns

From that dataset, I want a list of species from the genus *Passer* (Old World sparrows)

## RAM required {.center}

::::{.columns}

:::{.column width="49%"}

**pandas**

132 + 205500208 * 50 = \
10,275,010,532 bytes \
**≈ 10 GB**

:::

:::{.column width="2%"}
:::

:::{.column width="49%"}

**Polars**

12,291,024,224 bytes \
**≈ 12 GB**

:::

::::

Let's image that you have only 16 GB of RAM on your machine

Running a browser and a few small applications take about 6 GB out of that. This dataset is already too big to process on your laptop

If you need to play with the raw data or a larger dataset such as the [eBird dataset](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/4fa7b334-ce0d-4e88-aaae-2e0c138d049e) of 1,775,781,186 rows and 50 columns (**CSV file of 3 TB for the raw data**), you will need A LOT of memory if you use pandas

## Options {.center}

In pandas or Polars:

- you can select only the columns you are interested
- you can read and process the file in chunks and try to combine the results

If you only need to run queries on the data however, the Polars Lazy API is the answer

## Create a LazyFrame {.center}

You can create a LazyFrame (no impact on memory, LazyFrame returned instantly):

```{python}
df_pl_lazy = pl.scan_csv("sa_birds.csv", separator="\t")
```

Run a query on it (no impact on memory, LazyFrame returned instantly):

```{python}
passer_df_lazy = df_pl_lazy.filter(
    pl.col("genus") == "Passer"
).select(pl.col("species")).unique()
```

## Collect result {.center}

Now, you collect the result (this uses memory and takes time to compute) and turn the DataFrame into a Python list:

```{python}
passer_ls = passer_df_lazy.collect().get_column("species").to_list()
```

Depending on the subset returned by your query, the memory usage will vary

Here, it is minuscule (79 bytes):

```{python}
passer_df_lazy.collect().estimated_size()
```

## Et voilà {.center}

```{python}
print(passer_ls)
```

With this method, as long as the data itself fits on a drive and the queries don't return huge subsets of data, you can run queries on giant datasets such as the 3 TB [eBird dataset](https://www.gbif.org/dataset/4fa7b334-ce0d-4e88-aaae-2e0c138d049e) with a small amount of RAM

Note that the queries can contain complex expressions. What matters is the size of the subset you need to collect at the end
